21: FTP — Unencrypted file transfer, credentials exposed in transit
22: SSH — Common brute-force target; ensure key-based auth only
23: Telnet — Unencrypted remote access, plaintext credentials
25: SMTP — Open relay risk, spam abuse vector
53: DNS — Amplification and poisoning attack risk
67: DHCP Server — Rogue DHCP server possible on open network
69: TFTP — No authentication, unauthenticated file access
111: RPC — Remote procedure call, exploited in many vulnerabilities
137: NetBIOS Name Service — Network enumeration risk
139: NetBIOS Session — Windows file sharing, legacy, unencrypted
161: SNMP — Default community strings allow full device info leak
445: SMB — EternalBlue target, primary ransomware vector
514: rsh / syslog — Unencrypted remote shell or unauthenticated syslog
1080: SOCKS Proxy — May be used as open relay
1433: MSSQL — Exposed database server, often attacked directly
1434: MSSQL Monitor — SQL Slammer worm target
2049: NFS — Unauthorized file access on misconfigured exports
4444: Metasploit default listener — likely malware or pen test artifact
5900: VNC — Often configured with no authentication or weak password
5985: WinRM HTTP — Remote management, used by attackers for lateral movement
5986: WinRM HTTPS — Remote management over TLS
6379: Redis — No authentication by default, full data exposure
8080: HTTP Alternate — Proxy or dev server, often unencrypted
8443: HTTPS Alternate — Often uses self-signed certificates
9200: Elasticsearch — No authentication by default, data exposure
10000: Webmin — Remote admin panel, high-value target
27017: MongoDB — No authentication by default, many breaches attributed
